more the opportunity I saw.
When you're that early, naturally, there's going to be some things to work on, but it's all good.
It was still very positive, don't get me wrong.
But anyways, so I wanted to just start to hopefully help you understand the mindset of why a lot of folks come to us before we dive into the particular things we do from a use case perspective.
Like, what's the reason behind it?
What's the mindset of these people?
Now, of course, there's going to be some variability, but generally speaking, you know, with the pressures that have come, we have a lot of SaaS businesses as customers.
It's not 100% SaaS, but it is pretty heavy SaaS.
You know, with VC-backed companies.
There's a lot of pressure to grow, grow consistently.
You know, it's great that you may have hit one quarter.
Great, you've got to hit the next and the next and the next.
And there's all this pressure of being able to grow fast, grow repeatedly.
And that has very much been even more so true the past handful of years where efficiency of your dollars has been more and more of a focus.
And so that pressure of speed and efficiency from the speed angle that led a lot of businesses to invest in channels like paid search.
Paid search is really easy for you to, if you're not familiar, it's, you know, you're like bidding on different searches and running ads.
And it's just a really easy, quick way to capture demand, right?
People are searching for a thing, you're showing ads, you're capturing that.
And the problem is a lot of businesses get kind of stuck with that.
It can be very hard to progress how you drive revenue beyond that if you're just laser focused.
And, you know, then there's things like how do you grow when people aren't actively searching for you?
Like, what does that look like?
And so a lot of businesses would hire me in the past, like why I got hired at Homebase, to help them diversify.
They have a, you know, a fixed amount of dollars to some degree or another.
There's going to be some flexibility at times, of course, but how do they, from that, more reliably drive more revenue from that?
And so if you're too stuck on paid search, that can lead to, you know, things that are out of your control, can start to affect your funnel.
Eventually, you're going to hit some sort of relative cap to a degree and to what you can drive.
And so a lot of businesses want to start to grow organically from things like organic search.
So, you know, those things.
How are you showing up in those searches?
But also what has become more and more prevalent today is as people are doing this research on things like a perplexity or ChatGBT, how are you showing up visibly there, right?
And there's a lot of other things that can kind of like go into that, but how you do that content is a huge, huge piece of that.
And so before I brought GrowthX in, I was spending quite a bit of money on just coordinating with freelancers, internal team, like what content are we writing, why?
And it just took like a lot of time and effort just to do like that coordination and then to actually get it out to the freelancers and then review their first drafts, make revisions to it, etc.
It's just a whole lot of overhead and I had to manage a lot of other channels as well.
That is the reality of a lot of our customers face.
And so for them, it can be really appealing for us to come in.
And say, hey, end-to-end, we will help build the right strategy for you.
We'll help execute on the content.
We'll be able to execute on it a lot faster than you likely were before.
And if we can hit that right quality bar for you, I mean, it's pretty hard.
It's kind of like a no-brain when you break down the math and the speed.
For customers that have felt that pain that I was describing, it becomes like, oh, my God, like, this is such a huge unlock.
And that's kind of like how I had thought about it at time.
And this is part of the reason why we've had such strong growth is there's just such a good fit for, like, the need in the market, the present state.
And people, you know, of course, the fact that we leverage AI is quite compelling to folks.
And so that's a little bit of, like, you know, the mindset and kind of what we're executing.
And so into, like, particularly, like, how we do that from a use case standpoint, there's a lot of different kind of, like, ways.
You can think about building an organic engine, right?
I'm trying to find this slide in this massive deck.
I know it's in here somewhere.
I'm going to just have to create my own version of this so I don't have to scroll endlessly.
Come on.

So there's a lot of different kind of places where you can, it's surface areas, if you will, of like where you can create content to build these organic engines.
And the way we like to think about it is, first, we need to understand our customers, businesses, of course, and their products.
But the personas that they're selling into as deeply as possible, like, you know, what motivates them?
What are their pain points, you know, their opportunities, et cetera?
How does that translate to varying questions and things of that nature that they're going to have?
And how can we create content that's really, really amazing answers to those questions?

If we can do that really well, you can build a lot of brand equity, you can build a lot of compounding engagement that should translate to them being able to generate demand and revenue from their content at a level beyond paid ads that it's just hard to beat.
Now, to build that, though, takes some time, takes a lot of strategic thinking and effort.
And so what we're really trying to do as much as possible, as Marcel likes to call it, is plant as many seeds as possible.
And as we start to learn from these different hypotheses, if you will, that we've put out there, how does that help us inform what is going to work strategically for them?
And so with AI, this is actually one of the really compelling things that we can now do is help.
The Fathom we can take a hypothesis, get it out into market, and then get feedback from that.
In the past, and what I explained before, how I was doing this with pure human-led before, that would take quite a while.
That could take multiple quarters before I have something that I thought I need to get it out there, and did this work or not?
If I can learn faster, that helps me grow faster.

And so, as we think about it in terms of the different types of content that we can put out there, and just that mindset, what we've found is, you know, there's a lot of customers that will come to us with all sorts of different opportunities and things they want to do with content, and that's all very exciting and things we want to do.
But we need to be careful with, like, what we sign up for.
As any startup, you know, we need to have a level of focus and ensure that we are setting these engagements up for success.
If we do everything super, super custom, it's...

going to be hard for us to get to this repeatable state of training our team on the right processes, of having the tech to support them, you know, just all the different layers that go into building something repeatable, right?
And so what that has meant for us is that starting out with editorial content has been a really good place for the vast majority of our engagements.
What do I mean by that?
That's longer form content.
You know, this could be things like blogs.

It doesn't have to be a blog, but the mindset here is that, you know, there is a deep perspective, again, that we're developing on the personas, the topic, what do they care about, and then have a long form piece that is speaking to that.
What are those different questions?
If you think about that person, if you think about that topic, what kind of things do they care about?
And are we covering that comprehensively?
And so what goes into that is a lot of...
There's minutes.
You know, how should a good piece flow?
What's the tone and voice?
What's the right structure?
So a lot of our processes, our workflows, things of that nature are developed around trying to execute that at a high level.
The benefit of this as well is we can continue to, you know, as we dial in things like tone, voice, what topics are resonating or not resonating with their audience.
There's just all these like rich learnings that can come from that.

And as we can take those learnings, that's what we mean by when we say like content to distribution to conversion, it should open up paths for us in terms of as we start to get more signal back of what's working, what's not.
How do we extend the distribution and get more value from that, whatever that angle is?
Maybe it's, you know, this particular topic is resonating with an audience, where else can we go find that audience?
sense.
And it can.
Continue to engage them with that theme.
As we continue to engage them, how do we start to convert them into revenue?
the end of the day, like, all this content is the intention for this is for them to realize revenue from this.
So if we're not converting that, eventually that's going to be something where our customers are going to come back and be like, this is all fine.
Like, created a lot of content.
It's good.
We got some traffic.
But if they're not doing conversions at the end of the day, most customers are not going to be too happy.
And so, you know, what we're really trying to do is, like, understand how we create this, like, comprehensive engine for them.
And starting with editorial content, we found is just an easier way to get there.
That doesn't mean, like, 100% of the time that's all we'll ever do.
It's just that's our preference.
That's where we want to start.

What does that style of engagement look like?

don't know if it's a bit off topic, but connecting with the project, the project that I am onboarding and I'm going to work.

to provide the visibility that you mentioned on the platforms.

Yeah, it's an interesting challenge, right?

So how a lot of businesses have had to operate is how do you take this type of content, get it out there as much as possible, rank as prominently as possible, on things like Google and generate traffic and this and that.
And that's still true.

I think the thing to keep in mind here that I'm finding I need to remind our customers about and perhaps even our team is, while GEO is absolutely a huge topic right now, something we need to pay attention to, experiment around, evolve, we also can't miss the perspective that, like, still the vast...
The vast majority of traffic is coming from SEO, but this is going to shift more and more and more.

So it presents this interesting challenge where there's a very defined set of ways, to a degree, that's a bit of an exaggeration, but a common set of patterns to how to think about optimizing for SEO, measuring its performance, and this and that.

So we still have to do that today with our customers, but then also we need to think about how does that take shape in GEO, and how do we optimize that content in a way that maybe works for both channels?
Where are the trade-offs?
What does that look like?
There's a lot of hot takes out there of what works, what doesn't.
Frankly, a lot of people don't know.

There's certain things for sure, like things like FAQs, brand mentions seem to certainly matter, how you structure content, and again, this kind of goes back to FAQs a little bit, but like question-answer pairings, putting content, you know, like.
Things that are kind of almost like the key takeaway, if you will, the TLDR, whatever it is, like prominently towards the top, a lot of these LLMs are like quite lazy.
So, you know, how can you make it easy for them to like surface the key points and extract them?
So there's definitely things we're learning.
But then, you know, there is this set of challenges of, okay, you can measure your visibility in mentions and this and that.

But I could honestly do a whole thing of like ranting of like all the different like challenges related to that, you know, when you're extracting via the API or going straight to the source and how much it can change day to day, whatever it may be.
But just your visibility is just like one layer, right?
And so like, you know, what does that mean?
What happens afterwards if your visibility increases?
And I'd be very interested to see if we can find a way to continue to help our customers think through that more.
I've been talking to.
Them about it.
So what I mean is like, as an example, like, not the perfect example, but like, how do you measure the performance of a billboard?
Like, someone, like, you could do like, hey, here's like this code or something, and maybe like, they type it in, and like, cool, if you type in this code, then whatever.
But like, if you're driving past the billboard, like, most people aren't going to do that, right?
So like, how do you measure the performance of that?
And there's a whole variety of like, ways businesses do think about that geographically, and this and that, but more the point is like, that should start to like, affect other channels.
And so, you know, did they start searching for your brand more?
Did they come in direct and just type in your URL and go and did you see an uptick there?
And so there's some really interesting challenges beyond just like, pure visibility to consider.
And the last thing I'll mention, because like, honestly, I spend like, the rest of the time just talking.
About this is like, how do you start to connect what's happening?
And I think this is like what we've been talking about is a angle for us that can be compelling.
How do you connect what's happening from a visibility and trend standpoint to action in some way?
And it doesn't feel like a lot of these tools are doing that super well.
So like, you know, like what is gaining traction right now?
Visibility-wise, is there a common pattern?
Is it a particular subtopic or whatever it is, right?
Like there's just all these different like things that if you were like to do some sleuthing and have, you know, this and that, you might uncover patterns.
But like, frankly, like there's a lot of noise in there.
And so like that's a pretty theoretical good use case for AI, right?
To like start to like find the signal and the noise in there and help kind of like focus you on a few different things.
And how do you then connect that to some sort?
And it just feels like a lot of these tools don't do that.
I've been able to now, kind of like moving away from the sales side, getting back to the customer side again, like getting a bit more hands-on with GEO stuff.
And I just find that like, it's just like, it feels like you're searching for a needle in the haystack with these tools.
It just doesn't feel intuitive.
And I'm sure there's things I just need to learn and like pattern recognize and understand.
Like all the things I've been doing for SEO, I've been doing for years.
And so it's just like second nature.

thank you a lot.

So a couple of things I just want to, like, you know, break down for you all.
Like, I've talked a bit about editorial content.
You'll also hear programmatic come up quite a bit.
You may see this reference as PSEO.
What we talk about here, I find, like, a lot of folks that join, like, you know, maybe, like, a handful have experience in this.
But some of this may be, like, somewhat of a foreign concept.
So I just want to make sure you're kind of understanding what this means.
Essentially, think of this as a more, as the name implies, you know, going after something repeatable.
You know, typically it is going after searches that have a lot of variants to them.
So think of it as, like, people who are searching for, you know, like, like, there's, I want to buy a car, let's say.
Take care.
Okay.
I want to buy a car in California.
I want to buy this type of car in California, right?

Like all the different permutations of that, how I may think about structuring my content and website, capturing those variants is probably to set some sort of template and then repeatedly like create content for all those different searches.
So that's essentially kind of like what programmatic is.
I can show you like a couple of quick examples.
So here's something we did for Ramp.
Ramp is like one of our oldest customers.
There are a whole set of different variants around people who are searching for per diem rates across different states and cities and things of that nature.
What that basically means is like an allowance.

Admittedly, I don't understand this at the deepest level, but like as you're doing business travel, what you can, what's actually like, there's these different rates set by cities and states and things of that nature.
so that's...
Actually, it's something that Ramp has a feature for.
And so we're like, oh, that's interesting.
They have a feature for this.
There's a lot of people that are searching for it.
There is a lot of crappy websites ranking for this.
We can probably create a better content experience for that.
And so underneath this is all these different pages.
Think of this, again, this could be different cities or states.
And, you know, as we are building these type of experiences, we're just, we're setting some sort of template.
What that could look and feel like can be wildly different.
Like this almost looks like a blog.
But, you know, like this H1 is going to be very similar, if not near identical across each page.
But we'll swap out like the states, right?
You know, put reference to the state in places like this.
But some of the other content is going to be like somewhat different.
But then we are actually bringing in the different rates.
And sometimes...
So we like scraped different government websites, and then we created these pages, and then we created essentially just like what the key behind this is like, what is the intent of the user?
And this is true of all content that we're trying to create, like what's the intent and how do you create a good user experience that fulfills that intent?
And so, and then how do we do that in a repeatable way is a bit of the difference of PSEO.
So we certainly do this for a variety of customers.

The reason we want to be careful of leading with this, with most of our engagements, is there's just like some technical aspects of like standing this up, like setting up their CMS, adapting our workflows for this, cetera.
It's absolutely possible.
It just takes more overhead for our team to get going.
And we've just found like some of our customers that are like very heavy in this, at times it's like harder for us to get traction early on, whereas like editorial, it's easier for us.

So hopefully that helps just give you a flavor of like what PSEO is, but usually like our customers are going to be starting in this, these two areas, because think of it as like, we're just trying to figure out how do we get more qualified eyeballs to their website, the people they care about with engaging content and that it's like meaningful and engaging.
Some of this other stuff, to a degree, is like where we can start to do some of these other things around distribution and conversion.
And so, for example, we've been doing a few different newsletter things for our customers.
We stood up a newsletter for the Augment Code team.
And so we took some of the content that we created for them, and then we actually started to curate a newsletter that brings in that content, brings in industry news, does all this other stuff.
Now, we want to be careful when we do these newsletter things, because they can be like pretty like labor intensive.
One of the things I'm working on right now is I'm leading.
And this engagement with Udemy.

Why?

And hopefully a lot more on this side is where we want to start.
And then, oops.
And then as, you know, our customers ask about different things, or we see different opportunities, we'll certainly try to blend in other elements.
